hook.text: Mini-Neptunes are small gas-rich planets between super-Earths and Neptune in size. They have thick atmospheres over rocky cores.

buildup.text: They're typically 2 to 4 Earth radii. Kepler data shows they're one of the most common planet types in the galaxy.

discovery.text: There's a gap in the size distribution—few planets exist between 1.5 and 2 Earth radii. It's called the 'radius valley.'

twist.text: Radiation from the host star may strip atmospheres off smaller mini-Neptunes, turning them into bare super-Earths.

climax.text: Understanding this valley could reveal whether a planet keeps or loses its atmosphere—key for habitability.

punchline.text: Not quite Neptune, not quite Earth. A cosmic identity crisis in the radius valley.

quiz.question: What is the 'radius valley' in exoplanet science?
options: A gap in planet sizes where few planets between 1.5 and 2 Earth radii exist
A valley on mini-Neptunes caused by atmospheric erosion
The distance range where no exoplanets have been found
